input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names using built-in framework features.

In .NET 11 (which would be .NET 9's successor... actually .NET 11 doesn't exist yet as of my knowledge cutoff. Let me think about this.

My knowledge cutoff is early 2025. .NET 9 was released in November 2024. .NET 10 would be November 2025, .NET 11 would be November 2026. So .NET 11 doesn't exist yet in my training data.


Since the user is asking me to create this on May 14, 2026, I should work with what's likely available then. For the core requirement—using System.Text.Json with PascalCase naming—I need to check what naming policies are available. I know `JsonNamingPolicy.CamelCase` has been around since .NET Core 3.0, and `JsonNamingPolicy.SnakeCaseLower` was added in .NET 8, but I'm not certain about PascalCase support in the versions I'm aware of.

The key insight is that System.Text.Json uses property names as-is by default when no naming policy is applied. Since C# records automatically generate PascalCase properties from their positional parameters, the JSON output will naturally be PascalCase without needing an explicit policy. In C# 9, positional records like `public record Person(string name, int age)` generate properties that match the parameter names exactly—so `name` and `age` in lowercase. When serialized with the default System.Text.Json settings, the JSON keys would be lowercase too. To get PascalCase output like `Name` and `Age`, I need to apply a naming policy or use custom serialization attributes.

The built-in options are limited—there's no `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` in .NET 8 or 9 since C# properties are typically already PascalCase. I could use `[JsonPropertyName]` attributes on each property, but that requires manual annotation. Alternatively, I could check if a newer .NET version added this capability, though I'm not certain about that.

Let me just try creating a test project and see what approaches work. I'll check what .NET versions are available first, then experiment with `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` to see if it exists or if I need to implement a custom naming policy instead.

data.content: Good — .NET 11 preview 3 is available. Let me create the project and check what naming policies exist.
